Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place this semester. I tried to set aside time on weekends, but with 14 online courses, I often felt overwhelmed and just tackled whatever felt most urgent. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to work through practice problems or activities rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything because I just felt too drained emotionally to focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how I was feeling that week. I aimed for consistency, but life kept getting in the way—stress would build up and suddenly I'd be cramming before deadlines. When I was in a better headspace, I could stick to a more regular schedule, but those times felt rare this semester. The unpredictability of my own emotional state made it hard to maintain any real rhythm.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if grades had come back. I logged in pretty frequently—like almost daily—because I was anxious about missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I didn't have the energy to actually study. The notifications on my phone would prompt me too.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking social media. I'd wake up and log in with my coffee just to see what was happening. It became part of my morning routine, which helped me stay on top of things even when my motivation was really low. Having good internet access made this easy—I could check from anywhere.
Q5: The interactive activities and practice exercises were the most helpful for me because I learn best by doing things with my hands, not just reading. I also found the video lectures useful—I watched over 200 minutes of them—because I could pause and rewind when I didn't understand something. The peer interactions helped too; connecting with other students made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. I know I should have participated, but I just couldn't bring myself to post anything—it felt too exposing when I was already feeling sad and overwhelmed. I also sometimes skipped the supplementary readings because they felt like too much when I was already juggling so many courses. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to an assignment or exam. I needed to feel like it would actually help my grade because that was the only thing keeping me going some days. Also, if it was interactive or hands-on, I was more likely to engage because that's just how my brain works. Passive reading rarely felt worth it when I was already exhausted.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford emotionally or time-wise. Required activities had this weight to them—I had to do them or face consequences—so they got done even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff felt nice in theory, but when you're managing high stress and sadness, you just don't have the bandwidth for 'nice to have.' I wish I could have explored more, but survival mode kicked in.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from my tablet or printed notes when I needed to get away from screens. Sometimes I'd work through problems in a notebook because writing by hand helps me remember better. I also used YouTube tutorials and other websites to supplement what we learned in class. A lot of my real learning happened offline.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be working through practice problems in my notebook or reviewing downloaded slides, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with my roommate sometimes, which really helped me understand things. The LMS only sees when I click on something, not when I'm actually wrestling with ideas on paper or in my head.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the lecture slides or videos once, then study from them repeatedly without going back to the LMS. It was easier on my eyes and I could study without internet if I was at a coffee shop or somewhere with spotty wifi. One download could mean hours of studying that the system would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up and I was feeling really sad. The CBT sessions helped a bit, but there were weeks where just getting out of bed felt like an achievement. When my stress was at its peak, engaging with coursework felt impossible—I'd log in, stare at the screen, and feel paralyzed. Those were the hardest moments.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just go through the motions. I'd click through videos without really absorbing anything, or open readings and close them immediately. Competing responsibilities from my other courses made it worse—14 courses is a lot, and sometimes this one had to take a backseat even though I cared about it.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just feeling numb. I'd technically 'watch' something but couldn't tell you what it was about afterward. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts because they required me to put myself out there, and I just couldn't handle that vulnerability. I also avoided starting big assignments until the last minute because the thought of them triggered my anxiety. I knew it was self-sabotaging, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope with my stress levels.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage, even if I'd been avoiding the material for days. It wasn't healthy—the stress spikes were intense—but it got me to a 93% assignment completion rate. Without deadlines, I honestly don't know if I would have completed anything this semester.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it gave me a small boost to keep going. But waiting for grades was torture—I'd check the LMS constantly, which probably inflated my login numbers. Negative feedback sometimes made me spiral and avoid that type of assignment longer next time. The CBT sessions helped me process feedback better toward the end of the semester.
Q18: The course structure helped because it was predictable—I knew when things were due and could plan around it even if I procrastinated. Having clear modules made it easier to break things into chunks. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to give this one the attention it deserved. More flexibility might have helped during my low periods.
Q19: The video lectures being available to rewatch was huge for me—I could go at my own pace. The interactive activities suited my learning style well. But the lack of hands-on or practical components made some topics harder to grasp. Discussion requirements would have been challenging if they'd been mandatory, so I'm grateful they weren't, even though I know I missed out on learning opportunities.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in constantly out of anxiety, clicking through things, but not having the mental energy to actually produce work. High activity might just mean high anxiety, not high productivity. Sometimes you're desperately trying to engage but your brain just won't cooperate, so you end up with lots of clicks and nothing to show for it.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was okay even though I wasn't posting in discussions or always visibly active. A lot of my learning happened offline with downloaded materials or handwritten notes. Someone might look at my discussion posts—zero—and think I wasn't engaged, but I was working hard in ways that didn't register. The LMS doesn't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, how often, what they click on. That's useful for spotting someone who's disappeared entirely. But it misses the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the clicks, and all the offline work. It can't tell if someone watched a video while crying or if they truly understood what they read. It's quantity, not quality.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections to other things you know. For me, real engagement feels like being present—not just physically clicking through things, but mentally invested. This semester, I had moments of real engagement, but also many moments of just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with significant sadness throughout the semester, or that I was in CBT sessions trying to manage my mental health while keeping up with coursework. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying from printed notes or the times I helped classmates understand concepts in person. It definitely wouldn't capture how hard I fought to pass this course despite everything working against me internally.
